column1: #deck:Corebook::Neuro::Brain::Primary Effects of CNS Trauma::Subacute Subdural Hematoma

column5:
Subacute subdural hematoma

Keyword findings:
crescentic subdural hematoma
local mass effect
bridging vein
developing membranes
3-day to 3-week age window

Key discriminator: the graphic ties subacute SDH to a crescentic extraaxial subdural collection from bridging-vein injury with membrane formation.
Why it matters: this is the mechanism scaffold for why older adults can develop SDH after minor trauma and why membrane behavior matters in late/unstable collections.

Differential:
Subdural hygroma
Subdural effusion
Subdural empyema
column21:
Subacute Subdural Hematoma
Source Basis: RadPrimer + STATdx master source; Core support limited to core_evidence.txt. Core evidence supports crescentic extraaxial SDH beneath dura, crossing cranial sutures, subacute isodense SDH around 1-3 weeks, and CT clues of mass effect/white-matter buckling/apparently thickened cortex.

High-yield frame: sSDH is about 3 days to 3 weeks old in the fused RadPrimer/STATdx source. It may become isodense to cortex, so detection depends on crescentic extraaxial location, compressed sulci, displaced CSF/cortical veins, gray-white interface buckling, mixed density/rebleeding, and membrane behavior.

column15: What CT-density evolution pitfall is shown for SDH?
column16:
RadPrimer/STATdx state that SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day.
By 7-10 days, blood becomes isodense with cortex.
By about 10-14 days, it becomes hypodense relative to adjacent brain.
Exam pivot: in the subacute window, the dangerous miss is looking only for bright acute blood instead of mass effect, buckled gray-white interface, compressed sulci, and displaced CSF/veins.

column5:
Subacute subdural hematoma

Keyword findings:
subacute right SDH
almost exactly the same attenuation as underlying gray matter
displaced gray-white matter interface toward midline

Key discriminator: Isodense right convexity collection detected by interface shift.
Why it matters: subacute SDH may be cortex-like or CSF-like on parts of the exam, so the reliable move is mass effect, displaced interfaces/CSF or veins, and membrane or blood-product behavior.

column11: Why are older adults predisposed to subdural hematoma after minor trauma?
column12:
Core and RadPrimer/STATdx tie SDH formation to tearing/stretching of cerebral bridging veins. In older adults, atrophic involutional change enlarges CSF spaces, stretching veins as they traverse the subdural space; minor trauma can then tear them and produce crescentic blood beneath the dura.
Imaging pivot: the collection is extraaxial/subdural, can cross sutures, and may create mass effect even when density becomes hard to see.

column11: Why can a subacute subdural hematoma become nearly invisible on noncontrast CT?
column12:
RadPrimer/STATdx state that SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day, become isodense with cortex by 7-10 days, and become hypodense by about 10-14 days. Core evidence separately supports isodense SDH as a subacute-phase finding around 1-3 weeks.
Imaging pivot: the diagnosis shifts from bright blood to indirect signs: mass effect, white-matter/gray-white buckling, apparently thickened cortex, compressed sulci, displaced CSF dots, and displaced cortical veins.

column13: What is the trap when a noncontrast CT shows no obvious hyperdense extraaxial blood, and what safety check prevents the miss?
column14:
Trap: Mistaking an isodense subacute SDH for normal cortex or mild cortical thickening because it is isoattenuating to gray matter.
Safety check: Actively inspect for increased mass effect, white matter/gray-white interface buckling, compressed sulci, and inwardly displaced CSF dots/cortical veins.
Key discriminator: Mass effect and displaced interfaces reveal the crescentic subdural collection even when attenuation matches cortex.
Why it happens: Subacute blood becomes isodense to cortex, so density contrast disappears before the mass-effect clues disappear.

column11: What pathology/membrane process explains subacute SDH enlargement and recurrence?
column12:
RadPrimer/STATdx describe sSDH as partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane. The outer membrane is tied to enlargement and repetitive hemorrhage; the inner membrane is tied to liquefaction of the SDH.
The fused source also describes a delayed hypersensitivity component with antigen release during clot liquefaction and antigen-presenting cells in the neomembrane.
Imaging pivot: enhancing or thick outer membrane is not just decoration; it marks the biologically active membrane compartment associated with instability/rebleeding.
